coredata.dc:title: A highly selective and sensitive “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for detecting Hg(II) based on Au/N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Alan
Xu, Qinhai
Zhao, Kun
Li, Zhenjiang
Liang, Jun
Li, Qingdang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a one-step hydrothermal tactics is developed for the preparing gold and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots by using folic acid, glycerol and chloroauric acid as raw materials. The as-prepared Au/N-CQDs with the average size of 4.01±1nm had both crystalline and amorphous carbon. Au/N-GQDs exhibited the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior with the maximum excitation and emission at 355 and 450nm, respectively. Furthermore, Au/N-GQDs were an excellent fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg(II) based on the fluorescence quenching effect, Au/N-CQDs showed high sensitivity for detecting of Hg(II) with a detection limit of 0.118μM and the linear relationship ranged from 0 to 41.86μM. The results of the selectivity experiments illustrated that the fluorescent probe was particular for Hg(II) and it was not interfered by high concentrations other metal cations and anions. When EDTA-2Na or I− was added, the fluorescence intensity of Au/N-GQDs containing Hg(II) turned on and increased to 90% of the blank sample. More importantly, the fluorescent probe based on Au/N-CQDs was successfully applied to the detection of Hg(II) in real sample. This work not only provides a novel method for the synthesis of Au/N-CQDs, but also provides a promising sensing platform for monitoring of Hg(II) in the environmental application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Gold
Nitrogen
Hg(II)
Fluorescence
Probe

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The measurement of quantum yield (QY) of Au/N-CQDs
Detection of Hg(II) ions in real sample
Sensitivity of Au/N-CQDs fluorescent sensor for Hg(II) detection
Selectivity and immunity of Au/N-CQDs fluorescent probe for Hg(II) detection
Detection of Hg(II) ion in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of Au/N-CQDs for Hg(II) detection.

(a) TEM image of Au/N-CQDs. (Insert: Diameter distribution histogram of Au/N-CQDs). (b) HRTEM image of Au/N-CQDs. (Inserts: Magnified image (I-1) of “I” Au/N-CQD. Magnified image (II-1) of “II” Au/N-CQD). (c) EDS of Au/N-CQDs. (d) High-resolution XPS spectrum of Au4f. (e) High-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s. (f) High-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s. (g) High-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s. (h) FT-IR spectrum of Au/N-CQDs.

(a) Excitation spectra of Au/N-CQDs at different emission wavelengths (430nm, 440nm, 450nm, 460 nm). (b) Fluorescence spectra of Au/N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (253nm, 270nm, 350nm) (Inserts: the photographs of the preparation of Au/N-CQDs solutions under the white (I) and UV (II, 365nm) light irradiation). (c) Emission spectra of Au/N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (340nm, 350nm, 355 nm, 360nm, 365nm, 370nm). (d) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Au/N-CQDs solution. (e) The fluorescence intensity of Au/N-CQDs (1/10) containing aqueous solutions at different pH.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum of Au/N-CQDs (1/20) in the presence of different concentrations of Hg(II) (The concentration of Hg(II) from top to bottom: 0, 4.83×10−6, 9.615×10−6, 1.905×10−5, 2.83×10−5, 3.738×10−5, 4.186×10−5, 4.630×10−5, 5.505×10−5, 6.36×10−5 and 7.547×10−5M) at λex =360nm. (b) The dependence of FL intensity on the concentration of Hg(II) in the range of 0 to 7.547×10−5 M. (c) The linear relationship between the F/F0 and the concentration of Hg(II) in the range of 0 to 4.186×10−5 M. (d) The fluorescence spectra of the recovery of Au/N-CQDs (1/20) containing Hg(II).

(a) Normalized fluorescence intensity of the selectivity of the Au/N-CQDs for Hg(II) detection in the presence of various metal ions at λex =360nm. (b) Photographs of the aqueous Au/N-CQDs containing various metal ions under daylight (left) and UV light (right, excited at 365nm). (c) Normalized fluorescence intensity of the selectivity of Au/N-CQDs for Hg(II) detection in the presence of various anions at λex =360nm. (d) Normalized fluorescence intensity of immunity of Au/N-CQDs by adding various metal ions into Au/N-CQDs (1/10) containing Hg(II) solutions. (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Au/N-CQDs solution and Au/N-CQDs solution containing Hg(II).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Au/N-CQDs (1/20) containing different concentrations of Hg(II) in tap water at λex =360nm. (The concentration of Hg(II) from top to bottom: 0, 9.00×10−6, 1.78×10−5, 2.65× 10−5, 3.51×10−5, 4.35×10−5, 5.17×10−5 and 5.98×10−5) at λex =360nm. (b) The dependence of fluorescence intensity on the concentration of Hg(II) in the range of 0 to 5.98×10−5 M in tap water. (c) The linear relationship between the F/F0 and the concentration of Hg(II) in the range of 0 to 4.35×10−5 M in tap water.
Comparison of different probes for detection of Hg(II).
Analysis of Hg(II) in tap water determined by the fluorescence probe based on Au/N-CQDs and ICP-AES methods.

head.ce:title: A highly selective and sensitive “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for detecting Hg(II) based on Au/N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, Shandong, PR China

College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Polymer Material Advanced Manufacturings Technology of Shandong Provincial, College of Electromechanical Engineering, College of Sino-German Science and Technology, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266061, Shandong, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Functional and Biological Nanomaterials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, Shandong, 266061, PR China

College of Materials Science and Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sino-German Science and Technology, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266061, PR China

Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Polymer Material Advanced Manufacturings Technology of Shandong Provincial, College of Electromechanical Engineering, College of Sino-German Science and Technology, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266061, Shandong, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a one-step hydrothermal tactics is developed for the preparing gold and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots by using folic acid, glycerol and chloroauric acid as raw materials. The as-prepared Au/N-CQDs with the average size of 4.01±1nm had both crystalline and amorphous carbon. Au/N-GQDs exhibited the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior with the maximum excitation and emission at 355 and 450nm, respectively. Furthermore, Au/N-GQDs were an excellent fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg(II) based on the fluorescence quenching effect, Au/N-CQDs showed high sensitivity for detecting of Hg(II) with a detection limit of 0.118μM and the linear relationship ranged from 0 to 41.86μM. The results of the selectivity experiments illustrated that the fluorescent probe was particular for Hg(II) and it was not interfered by high concentrations other metal cations and anions. When EDTA-2Na or I− was added, the fluorescence intensity of Au/N-GQDs containing Hg(II) turned on and increased to 90% of the blank sample. More importantly, the fluorescent probe based on Au/N-CQDs was successfully applied to the detection of Hg(II) in real sample. This work not only provides a novel method for the synthesis of Au/N-CQDs, but also provides a promising sensing platform for monitoring of Hg(II) in the environmental application.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is the source of life and one of the most precious natural resources [1]. However, with the development of industry, heavy metal ion pollution has become a critical worldwide issue for years due to the severe risks in human health and the environment [2]. The mercury ion (Hg(II)) is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions which can be easily bio-accumulated in the human body through the food chain, which has raised serious health concerns for decades [3,4]. Due to their participations in biological procedures of human beings, a series of diseases such as nerve disorder, perforation of stomach, intestines septum and renal failure were caused [5]. The water soluble divalent mercuric ion is one of the most usual and stable forms in mercury pollution, which cannot be destroyed when it has been released into the environment due to its nonbiodegradability [6]. Thus, it is significant to establish a selective and sensitive method for the detection Hg(II) ions [7]. In addition to traditional methods of ICP-AES, ICP-MS, SERS, LC, GC for the detecting Hg(II), colorimetric and electrochemical methods have also been developed in present. Razium A. Soomro et al. synthesizes Cyst-Cu NPs, which demonstrated very sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of Hg(II) ions in the range of 0.5μM–3.5μM [8]. Zhiqiang Zhu et al. report a highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for the detection of Hg(II) ions with the detection range of 0–100nM [9]. These methods are effective methods for the Hg(II) detection. In addition, fluorescent probes as effective methods to detect Hg(II) have also been of interest in recent years, which have the advantages of high sensitivity, specificity, fast response, and technical simplicity [10,11]. Ge et al. detects Hg(II) by mesoporous slilica [6]. Roy et al. utilizes DEAS-BHP to gauge Hg(II) [10]. Nevertheless, in order to get better detection performances, new fluorescent probes still need to be developed.

Recently, giving an overview of most nanomaterials, fluorescent probes based on carbon quantum dots are gaining more and more attention because of excellent physical and chemical properties and potential applications. Carbon quantum dots is a new kind of the member of carbon nanomaterials including nanodiamonds, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets, carbon quantum dots [12,13]. Carbon quantum dots are first discovered during the purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotubes fragments in 2004 [14]. Carbon quantum dots can be amorphous or nanocrystalline with sp2 carbon clusters. Some studies also suggest the existence of diamond-like structure formed by sp3 carbons [15]. CQDs emit bright blue fluorescence under the irradiation of ultraviolet light, which originated from band gap transitions or surface defects [12]. Due to the advantages of size-dependent and excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, good stability and easy preparation [16], many works have been carried out on the facile synthesis methods of CQDs and intensive studies of their photoluminescence properties [17]. Methods for synthesizing carbon quantum dots include hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, chemical ablation, electrochemical carbonization, laser ablation, microwave irradiation [18]. The hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) process shows clear advantages, in that it can generate a variety of the cheap and sustainable carbonaceous materials with attractive nanostructure and function for a wide range of applications [19]. Currently, by monitoring the fluorescence intensity changes under external physical or chemical stimuli, CQDs have been used to detect different ions [20]. Guo et al. detected copper ions using the carbon dot [21]. Gao et al. used carbon nanodots for detection of Ag+ based on the Ag+-induced enhancement of fluorescence [22]. In addition, to enhance the quantum yield and application capabilities of CQDs, doping Au, Ag, B, N, Si and other elements is a popular approach to tune the PL properties of photoluminescent materials [23]. CQDs doped with different elements contain a large amount of carboxylic and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces, endowing them with excellent water solubility and suitability for the subsequent functionalization [24]. CQDs doped with nitrogen element have also been used for the detection of Hg(II). Zhang et al. synthesizes N-CQDs for detection of Hg(II) [2]. Wu et al. exploits N-CQDs to detect Hg(II) [25]. At present, gold doped carbon quantum dots for the detection of Hg(II) have not been reported, as far as we know, but gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots (Au/CQDs) composite had been synthesized. Liu et al. prepared Au nanoparticles/CQDs composite as a photocatalyst for high-efficiency cyclohexane oxidation [26]. Liu et al. developed a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal sensor for arginine detection by inhibiting the growth of gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots composite [27]. However, the quantum yield and the detection capability of CQDs are still needed to be further improved. Co-doped is an effective way to improve fluorescence performance. When Au and N are co-doped into CQDs, the two elements have synergistic effects [28], which might effectively improve the quantum yield and the detection ability of the co-doped CQDs. For the moment, gold and nitrogen co-doped CQDs have not been reported in the literature. Accordingly, developing simple method suitable for synthesizing gold and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots to improve the quantum yield and the detection capability is quite a challenging research work.

Herein, a one-step hydrothermal method was developed to prepare gold and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Au/N-CQDs), for the first time by using folic acid (FA) as carbon and nitrogen source, glycerol (Gly) as carbon source and chloroauric acid (CA) as gold source. The as-prepared Au/N-CQDs with the average size of 4.01±1nm had both crystalline and amorphous carbon. Au/N-GQDs emitted blue fluorescence (FL) at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Based on the fluorescence quenching property, Au/N-CQDs as a promising sensing platform for determination of Hg(II) showed the high selectivity, excellent immunity, outstanding sensitivity, lower detection limit and a wider linear range. When EDTA-2Na or I− was added into Au/N-CQDs containing Hg(II), the “off” state of fluorescence of Au/N-GQDs containing Hg(II) was recovered (Scheme 1 ). More importantly, the fluorescent probe based on Au/N-CQDs is successfully applied to the detection of Hg(II) in real sample.

Folic acid (FA, C19H19N7O6) was purchased from Beijing Bai ling wei Technology Co. Ltd. Glycerol (Gly, HOCH2CHOHCH2OH) was bought fromTianjin Bodie Chemical Co., Ltd. Chloroauric acid (CA, HAuCl4·3H2O) was obtained from Tianjin Maisike company. Standard stock (1mM) solution of Hg(II) ions was prepared by adding Hg(NO3)·1/2H2O (purchased from Jiangyan Huanqiu Reagent Factory) into deionized water. Other solutions (1mM) were prepared in deionized water from the salts of FeSO4·7H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, MgSO4, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CdCl2·1/2H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, CoSO4·7H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, KCl, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, Na2SO4, NaCl, NaNO3, H2SO4 and NaOH, respectively. All chemicals were analytical grade and all aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was taken on a JEM-2100 microscope (Japan) operated at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was surveyed by a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (USA) operated at 150W. The binding energy was calibrated against the carbon 1s line at 284.8eV. Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted with a Nicolet is10 (Thermo Scientic, CaF wafer technique, USA). The content of gold was measured by Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP-AES) (Prodigy XP, USA). The fluorescence spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700, Japan). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Singapore). The lifetime of fluorescence was gauged by an Edinburgh FLS980 all functional steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The photographs of Au/N-CQDs solutions under UV light (365nm) irradiation were displayed by ultraviolet analyzer (Shanghai Kang Huasheng instrument manufacturing Co., Ltd.).

Au/N-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.1g of FA, 1ml of Gly and 1ml of CA were dissolved in 30ml deionized water. Then, the solutions were heated and stirred by a magnetic heating agitator at 80°C for 10min. The solutions were added to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50ml) and heated at 180°C for 12h. Lastly, the autoclave cooled down to room temperature naturally, the flaxen solutions were acquired. In order to remove the aggregate, the solutions were filtered with 0.22μm micro filtration membranes. Au/N-CQDs solutions were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The measurement of quantum yield (QY) of Au/N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield of Au/N-CQDs was determined by using a reference method at 350nm excitation wavelength [2]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 solution (quantum yield of 57.7% when excited at 350nm) was selected as a criterion by the following equation:
(1) Φ=Φs [(I/Is) (As/A) (n2/ns 2)]
Where “Φ” is the quantum yield, “I” is the fluorescence peak area at the maximum excitation wavelength, “A” is the UV–vis absorbance intensity, the subscript “S” represents the reference quinine sulfate, “n” is the different refractive index of solvent, the superscript “2” means the square of “n”.

The prepared Au/N-CQDs solutions were diluted 20 times using deionized water and named Au/N-CQDs solutions (1/20). The sensitivity of Au/N-CQDs for Hg(II) detection was confirmed by adding 60μl of Au/N-CQDs solutions (1/20) into 2ml of deionized water. Then, 10μl of Hg(II) (1mM) was orderly added into the mixed solutions. The concentration of Hg(II) in the mixed solutions was 0, 4.83, 9.615, 19.05, 28.30, 37.38, 41.86, 46.30, 55.05, 63.6 and 75.47μM, respectively. The prepared Au/N-CQDs solutions were diluted 10 times using deionized water and named Au/N-CQDs solutions (1/10). The selectivity of Au/N-CQDs for Hg(II) detection was measured by adding 0.9ml water (the blank sample) and 0.9ml of metal ions (Al(III), Ba(II), Ca(II), Cd(II), Hg(II), Co(II), Cu(II), Cr(III), Fe(II), Fe(III), Mg(II), Na(I), Mn(II), K(I), Zn(II) and Ni(II)) and anions (NO3 −, Cl− and SO4 2−) stock solutions (1mM) into 0.3ml of Au/N-CQDs solutions (1/10) under the same conditions, respectively. The immunity of Au/N-CQDs for Hg(II) detection were measured by adding 2ml water (the blank sample) and 2ml of other metal ions (Al(III), Ba(II), Ca(II), Cd(II), Hg(II), Co(II), Cu(II), Cr(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), Mg(II), Na(I), Mn(II), K(I), Zn(II) and Ni(II)) into 2ml of Hg(II) solution (10−3 M) and 1ml of Au/N-CQDs solutions (1/10), respectively. The aqueous solutions at different pH (1.86, 2.82, 3.69, 4.73, 5.75, 6.41, 7.01, 7.68, 8, 8.4, 8.4 and 9.97) were prepared by using sodium hydroxide and concentrated sulfuric acid, 0.5ml of the aqueous solutions at different pH and blank (deionized water) solution were added into 0.5ml of Au/N-CQDs, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700) at the excitation wavelength of 360nm. All solutions were prepared in deionized water at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg(II) ions in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of Au/N-CQDs used as a fluorescent probe for Hg(II) detection in real sample, tap water obtained from our lab with no further processing was selected as a real sample. The prepared Au/N-CQDs solutions were diluted 20 times using tap water and named Au/N-CQDs tap solutions (1/20). Hg(II) tap solutions were prepared by adding HgCl2 into tab water at room temperature. The sensitivity of Au/N-CQDs for Hg(II) detection in real sample was confirmed by adding 200μl of Au/N-CQDs tap solution (1/20) into 2ml of tap water, then, 20μl of Hg(II) (1mM) was orderly added into the mixed solution. The concentration of Hg(II) in the mixed solution was 0, 9.00, 17.8, 26.5, 35.1, 43.5, 51.7 and 59.8μM. The fluorescence intensity was recorded on a spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700) under the same conditions at excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

Fig. 1 a shows the TEM images of Au/N-CQDs dispersed in water. One hundred and twenty Au/N-CQDs were randomly selected to measure the particle size, and the particle size distribution histogram was shown in Fig. 1a insert, the average size of Au/N-CQDs was 4.01±1nm, which was much smaller than the literature reported value [2,16,29]. It could be seen from Fig. 1b, there are crystalline carbon (the “I” Au/N-CQD) and amorphous phases (the “II” Au/N-CQD) in HRTEM images of Au/N-CQDs [30]. As shown in magnified image (I-1), the lattice spacing of 0.186nm in the “I” Au/N-CQD is well consistent with the (105) facet of carbon (PDF # 26-1078), while in magnified image (II-1), there is no significant lattice spacing in the “II” Au/N-CQD. In addition, hydrothermal tactics used for the preparation of gold and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots have an advantage of novel, simple and a large number of Au/N-CQDs. Au/N-CQDs were further characterized by EDS, XPS, FTIR and ICP. EDS illustrates the presence of Au, C, N and O in Fig. 1c. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Au4f spectrum is shown in Fig. 1d, the peaks at 88.0 and 98.3eV illustrate the existence of the gold elements. The concentration of gold elements in Au/N-CQDs is determined as 4.7338ppm by using Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP), which also proves the existence of gold elements in Au/N-CQDs. It could see from Fig. 1e, the C1s spectrum is deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6, 284.8, 285.1 and 288.5eV, which corresponded to CC/CC, CO/CH, CN and CO/CN groups, respectively [2,25,31,32]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s spectrum shown in Fig. 1f is deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.3 and 531.6eV, which ascribed to CO and CO/OH groups, respectively, showing Au/N-CQDs contains a large number of oxygen functional groups. [32]. From the N1s spectrum shown in Fig. 1g, the three deconvoluted peaks at 399.5, 399.8 and 400.1eV are assigned to NC, CN/NH and CN/NH groups, respectively [33,31], which confirms that the nitrogen elements are existed. Fig. 1h is the FTIR spectrum of Au/N-CQDs. The bands at 3355, 2937/2883cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of OH/NH, CH bonds [33,32]. The band at 1710cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CC bond [34]. The stretching vibration of CO, CN bonds are at 1670cm−1 [35]. The band at 1420cm−1 is put down to CN stretching vibrations [36]. The bands at 1213, 1111/1043cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of CC, CO bonds [29], respectively. The results of FTIR illustrate that many functional groups are existed on the surface of Au/N-CQDs. Folic acid and glycerol not only provide carbon sources for synthesizing doped CQDs but also have a large number of functional groups, such as NH2, COOH and OH, which are beneficial to produce high quantum yield of CQDs and improve the solubility of CQDs in water [2,37]. As already stated, the results of FTIR are consistent with the results of XPS. It demonstrates that the surface of as-synthesized Au/N-CQDs are functionalized by multiple carbon-containing groups, oxygen-containing groups and nitrogen-containing groups, which have an effect on fluorescence intensity of Au/N-CQDs. Among the above functional groups, nitrogen-containing groups are very significant, which are very important for the formation of CQDs and have the prominent effect on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs [37].

As shown in Fig. 2 a, there are three peaks in excitation spectrum of Au/N-CQDs at 253nm, 270nm, 350nm when emission wavelength is at 450nm. Excitation spectra have blue-shift with the increase of emission wavelength. Au/N-CQDs generate fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths (253nm, 270nm, 350nm) displayed in Fig. 2b, when the excitation wavelength is 350nm, the fluorescence intensity of Au/N-CQDs was the highest among them, therefore, the excitation wavelength of Au/N-CQDs was chosen around 350nm. It could be seen from Fig. 2b inserts, Au/N-CQDs have excellent water solubility, which are flaxen solutions under white light (Fig. 2b insert I) and display the strong blue fluorescence at the excitation of 365nm (Fig. 2b insert II). In Fig. 2c, the fluorescence peak shifts from 455 to 440nm with the increase of excitation wavelength from 340 to 370nm, the maximum fluorescence intensity of Au/N-CQDs is at the emission wavelength of 450nm when the excitation wavelength is at about 355–360nm. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of Au/N-CQDs was chosen at 360nm. Emission spectrum also has blue-shift with the increase of excitation wavelength. By selecting quinine sulfate as a criterion, the quantum yield (QY) of Au/N-GQDs is calculated to be 8.6% according to Eq. (1). Fig. 2d is the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Au/N-CQDs solutions. There are three absorbance peaks at 234, 279 and 327nm, corresponding to the three peaks in the excitation spectrum. The peak at 234nm is ascribed to the π–π* transitions of the CC bond [38], the peak at 279nm is attributed to the n-π* transitions of the CO bond [25], the peak at 327nm is put down to the n-π* transitions of the functional groups on the surface of Au/N-CQDs [39]. The effect of pH (1.86, 2.82, 3.69, 4.73, 5.75, 6.41, 7.01, 7.68, 8, 8.8 and 9.97) on the fluorescence intensity of Au/N-CQDs is further explored. As shown in Fig. 2e, the fluorescence intensity is almost unchanged. Consequently, pH has no effect on the fluorescence intensity of Au/N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of Au/N-CQDs fluorescent sensor for Hg(II) detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the fluorescence intensity of Au/N-CQDs (1/20) decreased gradually in the presence of different concentrations of Hg(II) at λex =360nm. From Fig. 3c, the linear regression equation with a correlation coefficient of 0.996 illustrated a good linearity in the range of 0–41.86μM. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.118μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD=3Sk/k, Sk as the standard deviation and k as the slope). The linear relationship and LOD are wider or lower than those previously reported values in Table 1 . The fluorescence intensity of Au/N-CQDs were continuously measured for 11 times when the excitation wavelength was 360nm. The maximum fluorescence intensities are almost the same (RSD=0.17%), showing that the fluorescent probe based on Au/N-CQDs has excellent stability. The different concentration of Hg(II) (19.05, 28.30, 37.38μM) was added into Au/N-CQDs (1/20) and their maximum fluorescence intensities were acquired. Each sample was measured 5 times and the detection results were average values. The analytical results of Hg(II) determined by the fluorescence probe based on Au/N-CQDs were 20.20, 27.90, 38.01μM, respectively, which were consistent with the added Hg(II) concentration. It indicated that the fluorescent probe based on Au/N-CQDs had excellent reproducibility. The recovery of fluorescence of Au/N-CQDs solution (1/20) after adding Hg(II) (1mM) is measured by adding EDTA-2Na (1mM) or I− (2mM). Table S1 shows four groups of the experimental data. As shown in Fig. 3d, the “off” state of Au/N-GQDs quenched by Hg(II) can be turned “on”, when I− or EDTA-2Na (Na2H2Y) is added into the mixed solutions, the quenched fluorescence intensity is recovered to 94% and 90% of fluorescence intensity of the blank sample, respectively. The mechanisms of fluorescence recovery are shown in the Eqs. (2) and (3). According to Eq. (2), when I− was added into Au/N-CQDs containing Hg(II), Hg(II) reacted with I− to produce HgI2. Hg(II) reacted with Na2H2Y to yield HgH2Y and Na+ on the basis of Eq. (3).

Equations for chemical reactions were:
(2) Hg2+ +2 I− =HgI2.
(3) Hg2+ +Na2H2Y=HgH2Y+2 Na+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and immunity of Au/N-CQDs fluorescent probe for Hg(II) detection
ce:section.ce:para: It could be seen from Fig. 4 a and b, when Hg2+ is added into Au/N-CQDs solution, the fluorescence quenching is significantly, while the other metal ions display a slight fluorescence quenching effect, which could be negligible. The results of Fig. 4c show that NO3 −, Cl− and SO4 2− anions have no effect on the fluorescence quenching. It directly illustrates that Au/N-GQDs fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg(II) is highly selective. As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon is not obvious compared with that of the blank sample when Hg(II) and other metal ions (Ag(I), Al(III), Ba(II), Ca(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Cu(II), Cr(III), Na(I), Mn(II), K(I), Zn(II), Pb(II) and Ni(II)) coexist. It demonstrates that Au/N-CQDs fluorescent probe for Hg(II) detection is also immunity. Fig. 4e shows UV–vis spectra of Au/N-CQDs changes significantly after the addition of Hg(II) ions, the peaks at 234, 279, 327nm become smaller or disappeared, it illustrates that Hg(II) could influence the surface states of Au/N-CQDs which led to fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence lifetime of Au/N-GQDs is measured by time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method. The fluorescence lifetime (τ1/τ2) of Au/N-CQDs is 11.4711 (79.86%)/2.6653 (20.14%) ns, respectively [41], the total fluorescence lifetime is 9.6976ns demonstrated in Fig. S1. The fluorescence lifetime (τ1/τ2) of Au/N-CQDs containing Hg(II) ions is 9.3050 (87.22%)/1.6712 (12.78%) ns, respectively, the total fluorescence lifetime (t) is 8.329ns. The fluorescence intensities of Au/N-CQDs and Au/N-CQDs containing Hg(II) ions (F0/F) are 354.5/305.2 in Fig. S2. So t0/t is equal to F0/F, it indicates that the fluorescence quenching of Au/N-CQDs containing Hg(II) ions is dynamic quenching [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg(II) ion in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of Au/N-CQDs as a fluorescence probe for detecting Hg(II) in real sample, tap water obtained from our lab with no further processing was selected as a real sample. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, FL intensity decreases slowly with Hg(II) concentration from 0 to 59.8μM. There is a good linear calibration with a correlation coefficient of 0.997 (R2 =0.997) under the concentration range from 0 to 43.5μM in Fig. 5c. The result indicates that this fluorescent probe used to detect Hg(II) in real sample is still sensitive. When Hg(II) tap water solutions with the different concentration (17.80, 26.50, 43.50μM) was added into the tap water solutions containing Au/N-CQDs (1/20), the analytical results of Hg(II) determined by the fluorescence probe based on Au/N-CQDs and ICP-AES methods were listed in Table 2 . Each sample was measured 5 times and the detection results were average values. It could be seen from Table 2, the analytical results of Hg(II) determined by the fluorescence probe based on Au/N-CQDs are consistent with the analytical results of Hg(II) determined by ICP-AES methods, indicating that the fluorescence probe based on Au/N-CQDs has great potential in real sample.

In this work, gold and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal tactics for the first time. The as-prepared Au/N-CQDs with the average size of 4.01±1nm had both crystalline carbon and amorphous phases. Au/N-GQDs exhibited the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior with the maximum excitation and emission at 355 and 450nm, respectively. Au/N-CQDs showed high-selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Hg(II) with a detection limit of 0.118μM and a linear rang of 0–41.86μM. By adding EDTA-2Na or I−, the “off” state of fluorescence of Au/N-GQDs containing Hg(II) turned on, the fluorescence intensity was recovered to 90% of the blank sample. Most importantly, Au/N-CQDs were successfully applied to the sensitive detection of Hg(II) in tap water sample. It is believed that the present strategy may provide new insights and means for the detection of Hg(II) ions, showing a broad application prospect for detecting Hg(II) in the field of environmental and biological detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51672144, 51572137, 51502149, 51272117, the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2016EMB25, the Higher Educational Science and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. J16LA10, the Application Foundation Research Program of Qingdao under Grant No. 15-9-1-28-jch, Shandong Province Taishan Scholar engineering under special funding and Overseas Taishan Scholar engineering under special funding. We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Alan Meng received her Master’s Degree from Qingdao University of Science and Technology in 2009. Her research interests cover the functional materials preparation, characterization, growth mechanism and biological detection.

Qinhai Xu has been studying for his Master’s Degree at Qingdao University of Science and Technology since 2014, where his main research interests are in organic synthesis, fluorescence and biochemistry.

Kun Zhao has been studying for his Master’s Degree at Qingdao University of Science and Technology since 2014, where her main research interests are in construction and application of electrochemical sensors and biosensors.

Zhenjiang Li received his Ph.D. in northwestern polytechnical university, in 2003. He has published 90 papers and 8 patents. His research interests cover nanometer materials preparation and application in biological sensor.

Jun Liang has been studying for his Master’s Degree at Qingdao University of Science and Technology since 2015, where her main research interests are in electrocatalyst for the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction.

Qingdang Li is a professor,who has published a lot of papers in Springer and IEEE. His main research interests are in the development, design, implementation and optimization of micro electro mechanical system (NEMS/MEMS/MOEMS) devices.
